([/ˈlɪmərɪk/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-Limerick.oga "File:En-us-Limerick.oga") [*LIM-ər-ik*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key");[[5]](#cite_note-5) [Irish](/wiki/Irish_language "Irish language"): *Luimneach* [[ˈl̪ˠɪmʲ(ə)nʲəx]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Irish "Help:IPA/Irish")) is a city in western Ireland, in [County Limerick](/wiki/County_Limerick "County Limerick"). It is in the [province](/wiki/Provinces_of_Ireland "Provinces of Ireland") of [Munster](/wiki/Munster "Munster") and is in the [Mid-West](/wiki/Mid-West_Region,_Ireland "Mid-West Region, Ireland") which comprises part of the [Southern Region](/wiki/Southern_Region,_Ireland "Southern Region, Ireland"). With a population of 102,287 at the [2022 census](/wiki/2022_census_of_Ireland "2022 census of Ireland"),[[2]](#cite_note-cso2022-2) Limerick is the [third-most populous](/wiki/List_of_urban_areas_in_the_Republic_of_Ireland "List of urban areas in the Republic of Ireland") urban area in [Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland"), and the [fourth-most populous](/wiki/List_of_settlements_on_the_island_of_Ireland_by_population "List of settlements on the island of Ireland by population") city on the [island of Ireland](/wiki/Island_of_Ireland "Island of Ireland").[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) It was founded by Scandinavian settlers in 812, during the [Viking Age](/wiki/Viking_Age "Viking Age").

Map of Limerick

**Limerick** ([/ˈlɪmərɪk/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-Limerick.oga "File:En-us-Limerick.oga") [*LIM-ər-ik*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key");[[5]](#cite_note-5) [Irish](/wiki/Irish_language "Irish language"): *Luimneach* [[ˈl̪ˠɪmʲ(ə)nʲəx]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Irish "Help:IPA/Irish")) is a city in western Ireland, in [County Limerick](/wiki/County_Limerick "County Limerick"). It is in the [province](/wiki/Provinces_of_Ireland "Provinces of Ireland") of [Munster](/wiki/Munster "Munster") and is in the [Mid-West](/wiki/Mid-West_Region,_Ireland "Mid-West Region, Ireland") which comprises part of the [Southern Region](/wiki/Southern_Region,_Ireland "Southern Region, Ireland"). With a population of 102,287 at the [2022 census](/wiki/2022_census_of_Ireland "2022 census of Ireland"),[[2]](#cite_note-cso2022-2) Limerick is the [third-most populous](/wiki/List_of_urban_areas_in_the_Republic_of_Ireland "List of urban areas in the Republic of Ireland") urban area in [Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland"), and the [fourth-most populous](/wiki/List_of_settlements_on_the_island_of_Ireland_by_population "List of settlements on the island of Ireland by population") city on the [island of Ireland](/wiki/Island_of_Ireland "Island of Ireland").[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) It was founded by Scandinavian settlers in 812, during the [Viking Age](/wiki/Viking_Age "Viking Age").

The city straddles the [River Shannon](/wiki/River_Shannon "River Shannon"), with the historic core of the city located on [King's Island](/wiki/King%27s_Island,_Limerick "King's Island, Limerick"), which is bounded by the Shannon and [Abbey](/wiki/Abbey_River,_Limerick "Abbey River, Limerick") Rivers. Limerick is at the head of the [Shannon Estuary](/wiki/Shannon_Estuary "Shannon Estuary"), where the river widens before it flows into the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean"). [Limerick City and County Council](/wiki/Limerick_City_and_County_Council "Limerick City and County Council") is the [local authority](/wiki/Local_government_in_the_Republic_of_Ireland "Local government in the Republic of Ireland") for the city.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Geography and metropolitan area

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Geography and metropolitan area")]

Limerick is located on the River Shannon with four main river-crossing points near the city centre. To the south of the city is the [Golden Vale](/wiki/Golden_Vale "Golden Vale"), an area of rich [pastureland](/wiki/Pastureland "Pastureland"). Historically, much of the city's industry was based on this agricultural hinterland.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

On 1 June 2014, following the amalgamation of the separate local government areas of the county of Limerick and the local government area of the city of Limerick to form Limerick City and County, a new Metropolitan District of Limerick was formed within the united council, which extended the city area. The Metropolitan District includes the city urban area and extends outwards towards [Patrickswell](/wiki/Patrickswell "Patrickswell") in the west and [Castleconnell](/wiki/Castleconnell "Castleconnell") in the east.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The City Metropolitan Area however excludes city suburbs located within [County Clare](/wiki/County_Clare "County Clare"). At the [2016 census](/wiki/2016_census_of_Ireland "2016 census of Ireland"), the Metropolitan District of Limerick had a population of 104,952.[[12]](#cite_note-Municipal_District_Limerick-12)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Limerick](/wiki/History_of_Limerick "History of Limerick")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Limerick-King-Johns-Castle-2012.JPG/250px-Limerick-King-Johns-Castle-2012.JPG)](/wiki/File:Limerick-King-Johns-Castle-2012.JPG)

[King John's Castle](/wiki/King_John%27s_Castle_(Limerick) "King John's Castle (Limerick)") on the [River Shannon](/wiki/River_Shannon "River Shannon")

### Ancient and medieval history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Ancient and medieval history")]

*Luimneach* originally referred to the general area along the banks of the [Shannon Estuary](/wiki/Shannon_Estuary "Shannon Estuary") known as *Loch Luimnigh*. The earliest settlement, *Inis Sibhtonn*, was the original name for King's Island during the pre-Viking and Viking eras. This island was also called *Inis an Ghaill Duibh*, 'The Dark Foreigner's Island'. The name is recorded in [Viking](/wiki/Viking "Viking") sources as *Hlymrekr*.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

The settlement dates from 812; however, history suggests the presence of earlier settlements in the area surrounding King's Island, the island at the historical city centre. Antiquity's map-maker, [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy"), produced in 150 AD the earliest map of Ireland, showing a place called *Regia* at the same site as King's Island. History also records an important battle involving [Cormac mac Airt](/wiki/Cormac_mac_Airt "Cormac mac Airt") in 221 and a visit by [Saint Patrick](/wiki/Saint_Patrick "Saint Patrick") in 434 to baptise a [Dál gCais](/wiki/D%C3%A1l_gCais "Dál gCais") king, Carthann Finn. [Saint Munchin](/wiki/Saint_Munchin "Saint Munchin"), the first bishop of Limerick died in 652, indicating the settlement was a place of some note then. In 812 the Vikings sailed up the Shannon and pillaged the settlement, burned [Mungret Abbey](/wiki/Mungret_Abbey "Mungret Abbey") but were forced to flee when the Irish attacked and killed many of their number.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The [Normans](/wiki/Normans "Normans") redesigned the city in the 12th century and added much of the most notable architecture, such as [King John's Castle](/wiki/King_John%27s_Castle_(Limerick) "King John's Castle (Limerick)") and [St Mary's Cathedral](/wiki/St._Mary%27s_Cathedral,_Limerick "St. Mary's Cathedral, Limerick").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

In early medieval times Limerick was at the centre of the [Kingdom of Thomond](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Thomond "Kingdom of Thomond") which corresponds to the present day County Clare, the Kingdom also included North [Kerry](/wiki/County_Kerry "County Kerry") and parts of South [Offaly](/wiki/County_Offaly "County Offaly"). One of the kingdom's most notable kings was [Brian Boru](/wiki/Brian_Boru "Brian Boru"), ancestor of the O'Brien Clan of [Dalcassians](/wiki/Dalcassians "Dalcassians"). The word Thomond is synonymous with the region and is retained in place names such as [Thomondgate](/wiki/Thomondgate "Thomondgate"), [Thomond Bridge](/wiki/Thomond_Bridge "Thomond Bridge") and [Thomond Park](/wiki/Thomond_Park "Thomond Park").

### Late Renaissance/Early modern history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Late Renaissance/Early modern history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/DV405_no.235_Limerick.png/250px-DV405_no.235_Limerick.png)](/wiki/File:DV405_no.235_Limerick.png)

Limerick, painted in 1830

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/George_Street%2C_Limerick_%285691906290%29.jpg/250px-George_Street%2C_Limerick_%285691906290%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:George_Street,_Limerick_(5691906290).jpg)

Cannock's Department Store on O'Connell Street in the early 20th Century

The English-born judge [Luke Gernon](/wiki/Luke_Gernon "Luke Gernon"), a resident of Limerick, wrote in 1620 that at his first sight of the city, he had been taken by its "lofty buildings of marble, like the Colleges in [Oxford](/wiki/Oxford "Oxford")".[[16]](#cite_note-16)

During the civil wars of the 17th century, the city played a pivotal role, besieged by [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell") in 1651 and twice by [the Williamites](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England") in the 1690s. The [Treaty of Limerick](/wiki/Treaty_of_Limerick "Treaty of Limerick") ended the [Williamite war in Ireland](/wiki/Williamite_war_in_Ireland "Williamite war in Ireland") which was fought between supporters of the Catholic [King James II](/wiki/King_James_II "King James II") (Jacobites) and the Protestant [King William of Orange](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England") (Williamites). The treaty offered toleration to Catholicism and full legal rights to Catholics that swore an oath of loyalty to [William III](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England") and [Mary II](/wiki/Mary_II_of_England "Mary II of England"). The Treaty was of national significance as it ensured closer British and Protestant dominance over Ireland. The articles of the Treaty protecting Catholic rights were not passed by the Protestant [Irish Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Ireland "Parliament of Ireland") which rather updated the [Penal Laws](/wiki/Penal_Laws_against_Irish_Catholics "Penal Laws against Irish Catholics") against Catholics which had major implications for [Irish history](/wiki/History_of_Ireland "History of Ireland"). Reputedly the Treaty was signed on the Treaty Stone, an irregular block of limestone which once served as a mounting block for horses. This stone is now displayed on a pedestal at Clancy Strand. Because of the treaty, Limerick is sometimes known as the Treaty City. This turbulent period earned the city its motto: *urbs antiqua fuit studiisque asperrima belli* (an ancient city well studied in the arts of war).

The peaceful times that followed the turmoil of the late 17th century allowed the city to prosper through trade in the late 18th century. During this time Limerick Port established itself as one of Ireland's major commercial ports exporting agricultural produce from one of Ireland's most fertile areas, the [Golden Vale](/wiki/Golden_Vale "Golden Vale"), to Britain and America.[[17]](#cite_note-libraryireland.com-17) This increase in trade and wealth, particularly amongst the city's merchant classes saw a rapid expansion of the city as [Georgian Limerick](/wiki/Newtown_Pery,_Limerick "Newtown Pery, Limerick") began to take shape. This gave the city its present-day look, including the extensive terraced streets of [Georgian](/wiki/Georgian_architecture "Georgian architecture") townhouses which remain in the city centre today. The [Waterford and Limerick Railway](/wiki/Waterford_and_Limerick_Railway "Waterford and Limerick Railway") linked the city to the [Dublin–Cork railway line](/wiki/Dublin%E2%80%93Cork_railway_line "Dublin–Cork railway line") in 1848 and to [Waterford](/wiki/Waterford "Waterford") in 1853. The opening of a number of secondary railways in the subsequent decades developed Limerick as a regional centre of communications. However, the economic downturn in the European conflicts of the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") and Napoleonic eras, and following the [Act of Union 1800](/wiki/Act_of_Union_1800 "Act of Union 1800"), and the impact of the [Great Irish Famine](/wiki/Great_Irish_Famine "Great Irish Famine") of 1848 caused much of the 19th century to be a more troubled period.

### 20th-century history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 20th-century history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Limerick_arms.jpg/250px-Limerick_arms.jpg)](/wiki/File:Limerick_arms.jpg)

City arms on a [manhole cover](/wiki/Manhole_cover "Manhole cover")

The [Limerick boycott](/wiki/Limerick_boycott "Limerick boycott") was an economic boycott waged against the small Jewish community for over two years in the first decade of the 20th century. It was accompanied by a number of assaults, stone-throwing and intimidation, which caused many Jews to leave the city. It was instigated in 1904 by a Redemptorist priest, Father [John Creagh](/wiki/John_Creagh "John Creagh").

During the [Irish War of Independence](/wiki/Irish_War_of_Independence "Irish War of Independence"), the [Limerick Soviet](/wiki/Limerick_Soviet "Limerick Soviet") was a self-declared soviet that existed from 15 to 27 April 1919. A general strike was organised by the Limerick Trades and Labour Council, as a protest against the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army")'s declaration of a "Special Military Area" under the Defence of the Realm Act, which covered most of Limerick city and a part of the county. During the strike a special strike committee was set up to print their own money, control [food prices](/wiki/Food_prices "Food prices") and publish newspapers.

By the mid-20th century, Limerick was characterised by economic stagnation and decline as many traditional industries closed or left the city. However, there were some success stories. In 1942 [Shannon Airport](/wiki/Shannon_Airport "Shannon Airport") (located in County Clare, 20 km west of the city) opened for the first time offering transatlantic flights. In 1959, Shannon Airport enabled the opening of the [Shannon Free Zone](/wiki/Shannon_Free_Zone "Shannon Free Zone") which attracted a large number of multinational companies to the region. A long campaign for a third-level educational institute to be located in the city finally bore fruit with the establishment of [NIHE Limerick](/wiki/National_Institute_for_Higher_Education "National Institute for Higher Education") in 1969 which eventually became the [University of Limerick](/wiki/University_of_Limerick "University of Limerick") in 1989.

Limerick is the third-largest city in the Republic of Ireland (after [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin") and [Cork](/wiki/Cork_(city) "Cork (city)")), with a population of 102,287 in the urban area[[2]](#cite_note-cso2022-2)[[24]](#cite_note-24) and 104,952 in the metropolitan district, according to the 2016 [CSO](/wiki/Central_Statistics_Office_of_Ireland "Central Statistics Office of Ireland") census.[[12]](#cite_note-Municipal_District_Limerick-12) the [2011 census](/wiki/2011_census_of_Ireland "2011 census of Ireland") had reported a large population decline in central city areas due in part to the Limerick regeneration process where in these areas the largest reduction was reported nationally and also to outward migration following the collapse of the local and national economy from 2008 onwards.[[25]](#cite_note-25) The population of the Limerick [Larger Urban Zone](/wiki/Larger_Urban_Zone "Larger Urban Zone") as defined by the EU is 162,413.[[4]](#cite_note-Eurostat-4)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] The ethnic diversity in Limerick's population, which includes a large immigrant community, saw rapid growth during the [Celtic Tiger](/wiki/Celtic_Tiger "Celtic Tiger") and the following decade.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] As of the [2016 census](/wiki/2016_census_of_Ireland "2016 census of Ireland") the Polish community was one of the largest demographic groups in the Municipal District, with approximately 4,000 living and working in the area.[[12]](#cite_note-Municipal_District_Limerick-12) In late 2006, it was proposed that Ireland's first Polish bank could open in the area during 2007.[[26]](#cite_note-Polishbank-26)

Limerick has a large stock of [local authority](/wiki/Public_housing "Public housing") housing. Before the amalgamation of Limerick City and County Council, 41% of all housing within the old Limerick City Council boundary was [local authority](/wiki/Public_housing "Public housing"), which was the highest in Ireland. This figure is however no longer accurate given the larger metropolitan district of the city.[[27]](#cite_note-limerickleader.ie-27)

As of the 2022 census, the ethnic makeup of the city was 76.53% [white](/wiki/White_people "White people") total, including 67.47% white Irish and 8.53% other white people, 13.52% not stated, 5.48% Asian, 2.31% other and 2.14% [black](/wiki/Black_people_in_Ireland "Black people in Ireland").[[28]](#cite_note-28) The city's religious makeup in 2022 was 61.31% [Catholic](/wiki/Irish_Catholics "Irish Catholics"), 14.11% no religion, 13.91% not stated, and 10.66% other religions.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

## Government and politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Government and politics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/OConnell.JPG/250px-OConnell.JPG)](/wiki/File:OConnell.JPG)

[O'Connell Street, Limerick](/wiki/O%27Connell_Street,_Limerick "O'Connell Street, Limerick")

### Local government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Local government")]

Limerick city is under the jurisdiction of [Limerick City and County Council](/wiki/Limerick_City_and_County_Council "Limerick City and County Council"), which is based at [City Hall, Limerick](/wiki/City_Hall,_Limerick "City Hall, Limerick"). The council has responsibility for local services such as sanitation, planning and development, libraries, collection of motor taxation, local roads and social housing in the city. The council comprises elected [councillors](/wiki/Councillors "Councillors") with an appointed full-time CEO as the city (and county) manager. Local elections are held every five years and the councillors annually elect a Cathaoirleach or chairperson to chair the council. Limerick City and County council was the first local authority in Ireland to have a directly elected mayor. In May 2019, residents voted in favour of a proposal for a directly elected mayor. Elections were held in conjunction with local government and European elections on 7 June 2024. John Moran was subsequently announced as the first directly elected mayor.[[30]](#cite_note-30) Former well-known mayors include TDs [Donogh O'Malley](/wiki/Donogh_O%27Malley "Donogh O'Malley"), [Stephen Coughlan](/wiki/Stephen_Coughlan "Stephen Coughlan"), [Michael Lipper](/wiki/Michael_Lipper "Michael Lipper"), [Jim Kemmy](/wiki/Jim_Kemmy "Jim Kemmy") and [Jan O'Sullivan](/wiki/Jan_O%27Sullivan "Jan O'Sullivan").[[31]](#cite_note-31)

Throughout most of the city's history, from 1197 when it gained its first charter, Limerick City had its own local government authority, Limerick Corporation later known as [Limerick City Council](/wiki/Limerick_City_Council "Limerick City Council"). The council was one of the oldest in Ireland and was only exceeded in age by [Dublin City Council](/wiki/Dublin_City_Council "Dublin City Council"). In October 2012 the Government of Ireland published *Putting People First- Action Programme for Effective Local Government* which set out government policy for reforms across all the main areas of local government in Ireland. Among the recommendations was the merging of Limerick City Council with [Limerick County Council](/wiki/Limerick_County_Council "Limerick County Council"). The changes came into effect on 1 June 2014 following the implementation of the [Local Government Reform Act 2014](/wiki/Local_Government_Reform_Act_2014 "Local Government Reform Act 2014").[[32]](#cite_note-32)

By the 1960s onwards the city had outgrown its boundary considerably; the city boundary was only altered slightly and never reflected the overall urban area of the city. A limited boundary extension on the city's north side in 2008 enlarged the city boundary by 1,020 hectares increasing the city's area by almost 50% and raising the population by an estimated 7,000.[[33]](#cite_note-33) The previous boundary, encompassing 2,086 hectares, was delineated in 1950. Newer suburban districts such as [Dooradoyle](/wiki/Dooradoyle "Dooradoyle"), [Castletroy](/wiki/Castletroy "Castletroy") – including the [University](/wiki/University_of_Limerick "University of Limerick"), Gouldavoher, and [Raheen](/wiki/Raheen,_County_Limerick "Raheen, County Limerick") were continually administered to by Limerick County Council until the merger of the two authorities in June 2014. This local government structure in Limerick caused a number of inefficiencies, most notably in the area of planning. A number of suburbs such as Westbury and [Parteen](/wiki/Parteen "Parteen") to the north of the city are governed by [Clare County Council](/wiki/Clare_County_Council "Clare County Council").

Following the merger of the two authorities in 2014, a new Metropolitan District of Limerick City was set up that included the city urban area and also settlements close to the city such as [Patrickswell](/wiki/Patrickswell "Patrickswell") and [Castleconnell](/wiki/Castleconnell "Castleconnell"). The Metropolitan district returns 21 councillors to Limerick City and County Council which in total has 40 councillors returned from across Limerick City and County.[[34]](#cite_note-irishstatutebook.ie-34) The Limerick City Metropolitan District is divided into 3 local electoral areas: Limerick City East, Limerick City North and Limerick City South which elect eight, six and seven councillors respectively.[[34]](#cite_note-irishstatutebook.ie-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)

Following the [2024 local elections](/wiki/2024_Irish_local_elections "2024 Irish local elections"), [Fine Gael](/wiki/Fine_Gael "Fine Gael") is the largest party on the authority with 13 seats followed by [Fianna Fáil](/wiki/Fianna_F%C3%A1il "Fianna Fáil") with 10, [Labour](/wiki/Labour_Party_(Ireland) "Labour Party (Ireland)") and [Sinn Féin](/wiki/Sinn_F%C3%A9in "Sinn Féin") with 3 each, and [Independent Ireland](/wiki/Independent_Ireland "Independent Ireland") and the [Social Democrats](/wiki/Social_Democrats_(Ireland) "Social Democrats (Ireland)") with 2 each, and [Aontú](/wiki/Aont%C3%BA "Aontú") and the [Green Party](/wiki/Green_Party_(Ireland) "Green Party (Ireland)") with 1 seat each, and [independents](/wiki/Independent_politician_(Ireland) "Independent politician (Ireland)") and other parties the remaining 5 seats.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

### National politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: National politics")]

For elections to the [Dáil](/wiki/D%C3%A1il "Dáil"), Limerick City is in the [Limerick City](/wiki/Limerick_City_(D%C3%A1il_constituency) "Limerick City (Dáil constituency)") constituency. From 2011, the constituency boundaries changed in accordance with the proposals of the [Constituency Commission](/wiki/Constituency_Commission "Constituency Commission") and the subsequent [Electoral (Amendment) Act 2009](/wiki/Electoral_(Amendment)_Act_2009 "Electoral (Amendment) Act 2009"). This changed the electoral boundaries from [Limerick East](/wiki/Limerick_East "Limerick East") and [Limerick West](/wiki/Limerick_West "Limerick West") to [Limerick City](/wiki/Limerick_City_(D%C3%A1il_constituency) "Limerick City (Dáil constituency)") and [Limerick](/wiki/Limerick_(D%C3%A1il_constituency) "Limerick (Dáil constituency)"). Limerick city encompasses the city, the suburban areas of [Castletroy](/wiki/Castletroy "Castletroy") and as far east as [Murroe](/wiki/Murroe "Murroe"). It also includes part of North [County Tipperary](/wiki/County_Tipperary "County Tipperary"). The [Limerick](/wiki/Limerick_(D%C3%A1il_constituency) "Limerick (Dáil constituency)") county constituency takes in most of the rest of the county. For [European Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament") elections, Limerick is in the [South](/wiki/South_(European_Parliament_constituency) "South (European Parliament constituency)") constituency.

## Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Climate")]

Limerick's climate is classified as [temperate oceanic](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") *Cfb*). Met Éireann maintains a climatological weather observation station at [Shannon Airport](/wiki/Shannon_Airport "Shannon Airport"), 21 kilometres west-northwest of the city in [County Clare](/wiki/County_Clare "County Clare"). Shannon Airport records an average of 977 millimetres of precipitation annually, most of which is rain. Limerick has a mild climate, with an average daily maximum in July of 20 °C (68 °F) and an average daily minimum in January of 3 °C (37 °F). The highest temperature recorded was 32.0 °C (89.6 °F) in June 2018, and the lowest −11.4 °C (11.5 °F) in 2010. Limerick is the cloudiest city in the country, averaging only 1,295 sunshine hours annually, 3.5 hours of sunshine every day. There are on average 62 days of no recordable sunshine, 6 days of thunder, 22 days of hail, 32 days of fog and 11 days of snow per year. Shannon Airport is situated on the Shannon Estuary at an elevation of 14 metres.
