## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Hunt_Museum_Limerick.jpg/250px-Hunt_Museum_Limerick.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hunt_Museum_Limerick.jpg)

[Hunt Museum](/wiki/Hunt_Museum "Hunt Museum")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Belltable.jpg/250px-Belltable.jpg)](/wiki/File:Belltable.jpg)

[The Belltable Theatre](/wiki/Belltable "Belltable")

In 2014, Limerick became Ireland's inaugural *National City of Culture*, with a variety of artistic and cultural events occurring at locations around the city throughout the year.

### Art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Art")]

The [Limerick City Gallery of Art](/wiki/Limerick_City_Gallery_of_Art "Limerick City Gallery of Art") on [Pery Square](/wiki/Pery_Square "Pery Square") is the city's chief venue for [contemporary art](/wiki/Contemporary_art "Contemporary art") exhibitions. It is home to a permanent collection of [Irish art](/wiki/Irish_art "Irish art"), which shows works from the early 18th to 20th century. The gallery houses the National Collection of Contemporary Drawing founded by the artist Samuel Walsh in 1987. Limerick's major contemporary art event is [EVA International](/wiki/EVA_International "EVA International"), Ireland's Biennial of Contemporary Art.[[38]](#cite_note-38) EVA International, centered mainly in the Limerick City Gallery of Art, is curated by varying guest curators and includes contemporary artworks by both international and Irish artists.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

Other Limerick arts groups include Contact Studios, which provides individual studio spaces for visual artists; [Ormston House](/wiki/Ormston_House "Ormston House"), a meeting place for the arts; and Limerick Printmakers Studio and Gallery, which provides printmaking facilities, a venue for exhibitions and events and an education programme.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Theatre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Theatre")]

The Lime Tree Theatre officially opened at the [Mary Immaculate College](/wiki/Mary_Immaculate_College "Mary Immaculate College") campus in October 2012. The venue hosts theatre, music, comedy, traditional arts, schools' performances, and conferences.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

The [Belltable](/wiki/Belltable "Belltable") on [O'Connell Street](/wiki/O%27Connell_Street,_Limerick "O'Connell Street, Limerick") hosts local playwrights and drama productions.[[41]](#cite_note-41) The University Concert Hall located in the [University of Limerick](/wiki/University_of_Limerick "University of Limerick") is a 1,000 seat venue and provides a large venue for national and international acts to visit the city. Limerick is also the home of street theatre companies, including Lumen Street Theatre,[[42]](#cite_note-42) Impact Theatre Company,[[43]](#cite_note-43) and Bottomdog Theatre Company.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

### Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Literature")]

Limerick's literature scene is supported by projects like the Limerick Writers' Centre, which was established in 2008 and runs a range of writing activities in the city. Limerick natives include Pulitzer Prize-winning author Frank McCourt, and novelists [Kate O'Brien](/wiki/Kate_O%27Brien_(novelist) "Kate O'Brien (novelist)"), Michael Curtin, Kevin Barry and Donal Ryan. Poets from Limerick include Michael Hogan, Desmond O'Grady, and John Liddy.[[45]](#cite_note-45) The Limerick Literary Festival is held annually in honour of Kate O'Brien.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

The city is the setting for [Frank McCourt](/wiki/Frank_McCourt_(author) "Frank McCourt (author)")'s memoir *[Angela's Ashes](/wiki/Angela%27s_Ashes "Angela's Ashes")* and its [film adaptation](/wiki/Angela%27s_Ashes_(film) "Angela's Ashes (film)"). The Frank McCourt Museum situated in McCourt's former school on Hartsonge Street opened in 2011, and contains artefacts from the book.[[47]](#cite_note-47) Limerick has also been the setting or location for the dramas *The Real Thing* (2002), *[Cowboys & Angels](/wiki/Cowboys_%26_Angels "Cowboys & Angels")* (2003) and *Lost & Found* (2015), among other films.[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50)

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Music")]

The [Irish Chamber Orchestra](/wiki/Irish_Chamber_Orchestra "Irish Chamber Orchestra") and the Irish World Music Centre are both based in the [University of Limerick](/wiki/University_of_Limerick "University of Limerick"). The university has a thousand-seat state-of-the-art concert hall that hosts visiting performers. The city's music scene has produced bands such as [The Cranberries](/wiki/The_Cranberries "The Cranberries"), guitarist [Noel Hogan](/wiki/Noel_Hogan "Noel Hogan")'s MonoBand, and [The Hitchers](/wiki/The_Hitchers_(Irish_band) "The Hitchers (Irish band)"). Electronic musician Richard D. James, more commonly known as [Aphex Twin](/wiki/Aphex_Twin "Aphex Twin"), was born in Limerick in 1971. The Limerick Art Gallery and the Art College cater for painting, sculpture, and performance art.

The [Limerick International Band Championship](/w/index.php?title=Limerick_International_Band_Championship&action=edit&redlink=1 "Limerick International Band Championship (page does not exist)") is an annual competition featuring [marching bands](/wiki/Marching_bands "Marching bands") from Limerick, the rest of Ireland, and the rest of the world. It is typically held around just after [Saint Patrick's Day](/wiki/Saint_Patrick%27s_Day "Saint Patrick's Day"), with the bands parading through the city centre, and attracts large crowds.[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53)

Dolan's Warehouse on the Dock Road is a venue specialising in live music, established in 1998.[[54]](#cite_note-dolans-54) Baker Place was a nightclub that held mainly local underground nights until its closure in 2011.[[55]](#cite_note-55) In 2020, it was announced that the Dromkeen-based Shiloh Trust was seeking planning permission to convert the former premises of Baker Place into a bible school.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

### Other

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Other")]

* Limerick is also home to comedians [The Rubberbandits](/wiki/The_Rubberbandits "The Rubberbandits"), [D'Unbelievables](/wiki/D%27Unbelievables "D'Unbelievables") and [Karl Spain](/wiki/Karl_Spain "Karl Spain").
* The Crush 87 nightclub (formerly known as Trinity Rooms until its closure in 2010), was relaunched in 2013.[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58)
* Riverfest is a summer festival held annually in Limerick since 2004. The 2014 festival was held in May and had a record 80,000 visitors.[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60)
* As with several other towns and cities in Ireland, and some elsewhere, there is an annual [Saint Patrick's Day](/wiki/Saint_Patrick%27s_Day "Saint Patrick's Day") parade.
* The Daghdha Dance Company is a contemporary dance company that has adopted a renovated church in John's Square, adjacent to [St John's Cathedral](/wiki/St._John%27s_Cathedral,_Limerick "St. John's Cathedral, Limerick"), as a performance space).
* The [Fresh Film Festival](/wiki/Fresh_Film_Festival "Fresh Film Festival"), which is held each spring, includes films made by young people (7–18 years).
* The [Richard Harris International Film Festival](/wiki/Richard_Harris_International_Film_Festival "Richard Harris International Film Festival") is held every October.

## Humorous verse

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Humorous verse")]

A [limerick](/wiki/Limerick_(poetry) "Limerick (poetry)") is a type of humorous [verse](/wiki/Poetry "Poetry") of five lines with an AABBA [rhyme scheme](/wiki/Rhyme_scheme "Rhyme scheme"): the poem's connection with the city is obscure, but the name is generally taken to be a reference to Limerick city or [County Limerick](/wiki/County_Limerick "County Limerick"),[[61]](#cite_note-61) sometimes, particularly to the [Maigue Poets](/wiki/Croom,_County_Limerick "Croom, County Limerick"), and may derive from an earlier form of [nonsense verse](/wiki/Nonsense_verse "Nonsense verse") parlour game that traditionally included a refrain that included "Will [or won't] you come (up) to Limerick?"[[62]](#cite_note-62) The earliest known use of the name "Limerick" for this type of poem is an 1880 reference, in a [Saint John, New Brunswick](/wiki/Saint_John,_New_Brunswick "Saint John, New Brunswick"), newspaper, to a well-known tune.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Media")]

### Broadcasting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Broadcasting")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Radio_House%2C_Limerick_Live_95.jpg/250px-Radio_House%2C_Limerick_Live_95.jpg)](/wiki/File:Radio_House,_Limerick_Live_95.jpg)

Live 95FM building in Limerick

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Limerick_-_RTE_Lyric_FM_%28Robert_Street%29_%285771337206%29.jpg/250px-Limerick_-_RTE_Lyric_FM_%28Robert_Street%29_%285771337206%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Limerick_-_RTE_Lyric_FM_(Robert_Street)_(5771337206).jpg)

[RTÉ lyric fm](/wiki/RT%C3%89_lyric_fm "RTÉ lyric fm") building

[RTÉ lyric fm](/wiki/RT%C3%89_lyric_fm "RTÉ lyric fm"), a state-run classical music radio station and part of [RTÉ](/wiki/RT%C3%89 "RTÉ"), broadcasts nationally from studios in the city centre on Cornmarket Square which opened in 1994. Limerick's local radio station is [Live 95FM](/wiki/Live_95FM "Live 95FM"), broadcasting from 'Radio House', near the waterfront at Steamboat Quay. [Spin South West](/wiki/Spin_South_West "Spin South West"), owned by [Communicorp](/wiki/Communicorp "Communicorp"), broadcasts to Counties [Kerry](/wiki/County_Kerry "County Kerry"), [Clare](/wiki/County_Clare "County Clare"), [Limerick](/wiki/County_Limerick "County Limerick"), [Tipperary](/wiki/County_Tipperary "County Tipperary") and southwest [Laois](/wiki/County_Laois "County Laois") from its studios at Landmark Buildings in the [Raheen Industrial Estate](/wiki/Raheen_Industrial_Estate "Raheen Industrial Estate"). Student radio station, Wired FM, broadcasts on 99.9FM from [Mary Immaculate College](/wiki/Mary_Immaculate_College "Mary Immaculate College"). Wired FM also has studios at Limerick Institute of Technology. Limerick City Community Radio broadcasts on 99.9FM every Saturday and Sunday.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [University Hospital Limerick](/wiki/University_Hospital_Limerick "University Hospital Limerick") has a radio station on 94.2FM, but this can be heard only in the hospital and surrounding area. [West Limerick 102](/wiki/West_Limerick_102 "West Limerick 102") is broadcast from [Newcastle West](/wiki/Newcastle_West "Newcastle West"). The national broadcaster, [RTÉ](/wiki/RT%C3%89 "RTÉ"), has radio and television studios in the city, which are periodically used to broadcast programming from Limerick.

### Print

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Print")]

Several local newspapers are published in the city, including *[The Limerick Post](/wiki/Limerick_Post "Limerick Post")* and *[The Limerick Leader](/wiki/The_Limerick_Leader "The Limerick Leader")* and magazines include the *Limerick Event Guide*, *Business Limerick*, and *Limerick Now*.

## Places of interest

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Places of interest")]

See also: [Architecture of Limerick](/wiki/Architecture_of_Limerick "Architecture of Limerick") and [List of public art in Limerick](/wiki/List_of_public_art_in_Limerick "List of public art in Limerick")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/LimerickSunsetPotatoMarket.jpg/250px-LimerickSunsetPotatoMarket.jpg)](/wiki/File:LimerickSunsetPotatoMarket.jpg)

The [Sylvester O'Halloran](/wiki/Sylvester_O%27Halloran "Sylvester O'Halloran") Bridge

Limerick city is approximately 25 km from [Shannon Airport](/wiki/Shannon_Airport "Shannon Airport"). Tourist attractions in the city centre include [Limerick City Museum](/wiki/Limerick_City_Museum "Limerick City Museum"), [King John's Castle](/wiki/King_John%27s_Castle_(Limerick) "King John's Castle (Limerick)") (1210), [St Mary's Cathedral](/wiki/St._Mary%27s_Cathedral,_Limerick "St. Mary's Cathedral, Limerick") (1168), the [Hunt Museum](/wiki/Hunt_Museum "Hunt Museum"), [The People's Museum of Limerick](/wiki/The_People%27s_Museum_of_Limerick "The People's Museum of Limerick"), the [University of Limerick](/wiki/University_of_Limerick "University of Limerick"), Georgian houses and gardens and the [Treaty Stone](/wiki/Treaty_of_Limerick "Treaty of Limerick"). There are several seasonal tours, including [Angela's Ashes](/wiki/Angela%27s_Ashes "Angela's Ashes") walking tour of Limerick City, a historical walking tour, and kayaking tours along the [River Shannon](/wiki/River_Shannon "River Shannon").

The city centre is divided between the traditional areas of "English Town" on the southern end of King's Island, which includes King John's Castle; "Irish Town", which includes the older streets on the south bank; and the current economic centre further south, called "[Newtown Pery](/wiki/Newtown_Pery,_Limerick "Newtown Pery, Limerick")".[[64]](#cite_note-64) Newtown Pery was built in the late 18th century before the [Act of Union](/wiki/Act_of_Union_1800 "Act of Union 1800") and, unusually for an Irish city and unique in Limerick, is laid out on a [grid plan](/wiki/Grid_plan "Grid plan"). The [Limerick Museum](/wiki/Limerick_Museum "Limerick Museum") (formerly aka the Jim Kemmy Municipal Museum), is located in the Old Franciscan Friary in Henry Street. It contains displays on Limerick's history and manufactures.[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-66)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/St_Johns_Cathedral_Limerick_Ireland.jpg/250px-St_Johns_Cathedral_Limerick_Ireland.jpg)](/wiki/File:St_Johns_Cathedral_Limerick_Ireland.jpg)

[St John's Cathedral](/wiki/St._John%27s_Cathedral,_Limerick "St. John's Cathedral, Limerick")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Limerick-St-Marys-Katedral-2012.JPG/250px-Limerick-St-Marys-Katedral-2012.JPG)](/wiki/File:Limerick-St-Marys-Katedral-2012.JPG)

[St Mary's Cathedral](/wiki/St._Mary%27s_Cathedral,_Limerick "St. Mary's Cathedral, Limerick")

The Georgian core of the city, with its [Georgian architecture](/wiki/Georgian_architecture "Georgian architecture") in the Newtown Pery, was developed from the mid-18th century. This core includes O'Connell Street (George Street before independence) from the Cecil Street intersection running to the southwest end of the Crescent, and southeast to Pery Street including Glentworth Street and Barrington Street. Other buildings of architectural note in the city are [St John's Cathedral](/wiki/St._John%27s_Cathedral,_Limerick "St. John's Cathedral, Limerick"), designed by the notable Victorian architect, [Philip Charles Hardwick](/wiki/Philip_Charles_Hardwick "Philip Charles Hardwick"). St Mary's Cathedral, at over 800 years old, is one of the oldest in Ireland. St John's Cathedral is more modern.

The [Hunt Museum](/wiki/Hunt_Museum "Hunt Museum") is based in the historic 18th-century former Custom House. The museum was established to house a collection of approximately 2000 works of art and antiquities formed by [John and Gertrude Hunt](/wiki/John_Hunt_(antiquarian) "John Hunt (antiquarian)") during their lifetimes.

The main park in Limerick is the [People's Park](/wiki/People%27s_Park_(Limerick) "People's Park (Limerick)") which was opened in 1877 in memory of Richard Russell, a prominent local businessman. The park is dominated by the Thomas Spring Rice memorial (MP for the city 1820–1832) and has a large collection of mature [deciduous](/wiki/Deciduous "Deciduous") and evergreen trees.

Limerick city centre changed significantly with the construction of several modern high-rise buildings in the early 2000s, in particular as part of the regeneration of the former docks area along the Dock Road. The suburban areas, where the majority of the population now live, have grown out from the centre along the main roads to Ennis (North Circular and Ennis Road areas/[Caherdavin](/wiki/Caherdavin "Caherdavin")), Dublin ([Castletroy](/wiki/Castletroy "Castletroy") and the university) and Cork (Ballinacurra/Dooradoyle/Raheen). Suburban houses are generally two-floor semi-detached homes for single families. These were built from the 1950s onwards in large estates by government projects and commercial developments, although there are many examples of Edwardian and older 1930s suburban homes on the main suburban thoroughfares leading towards the city (North & South Circular, Ballinacurra Road, O'Connell Avenue).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/O%27Connell_Monument_to_Daniel_O%27Connell_at_O%27Connell_Avenue%2C_Limerick_City.jpg/250px-O%27Connell_Monument_to_Daniel_O%27Connell_at_O%27Connell_Avenue%2C_Limerick_City.jpg)](/wiki/File:O%27Connell_Monument_to_Daniel_O%27Connell_at_O%27Connell_Avenue,_Limerick_City.jpg)

O'Connell Monument to Daniel O'Connell at The Crescent

Limerick city's nightlife is supported by a number of nightclubs and pubs which offer music. For example, Dolan's Warehouse is a small music venue that hosts local, national, and international folk, indie, jazz, rock, and traditional Irish music acts.[[54]](#cite_note-dolans-54)

In County Limerick, [Adare](/wiki/Adare "Adare") village and the Foynes Flying Boat Museum, approximately 35 km (22 miles/30 minutes) from Limerick City along the scenic coastal N69 route from Limerick to Tralee, are also attractions. [Bunratty Castle](/wiki/Bunratty_Castle "Bunratty Castle") in County Clare is another local attraction, and is situated 15.4 km north of the city.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Economy")]

Main article: [Economy of Limerick](/wiki/Economy_of_Limerick "Economy of Limerick")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/RiverPoint.JPG/250px-RiverPoint.JPG)](/wiki/File:RiverPoint.JPG)

[Riverpoint](/wiki/Riverpoint "Riverpoint"), Limerick's tallest building.

Limerick is in the region dubbed "[the Midwest](/wiki/Mid-West_Region,_Ireland "Mid-West Region, Ireland")". Also known as the "Shannon Region", this is primarily an economic and social concept. The region encompasses [County Limerick](/wiki/County_Limerick "County Limerick"), County Clare, North [County Tipperary](/wiki/County_Tipperary "County Tipperary"), Northwest [County Kerry](/wiki/County_Kerry "County Kerry") and south [County Offaly](/wiki/County_Offaly "County Offaly"), with its focal point centred on Limerick and its environs within an 8-kilometre (5 mi) radius.

The city's economic development has been driven in part by the [University of Limerick](/wiki/University_of_Limerick "University of Limerick"), [Limerick Institute of Technology](/wiki/Limerick_Institute_of_Technology "Limerick Institute of Technology"), [Shannon Airport](/wiki/Shannon_Airport "Shannon Airport") in County Clare and [Shannon Development](/wiki/Shannon_Development "Shannon Development") (an economic development agency), whose precursor was SFADCO (Shannon Free Airport Development Company), an economic agency that provided tax incentives to companies locating in the area surrounding Shannon Airport. Limerick Chamber of Commerce, a representative body for businesses in the region, celebrated its bicentennial/bicentenary in 2015. As of 2015, Limerick had the highest disposable income per person in Ireland outside [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin").[[67]](#cite_note-67)

Historically Limerick was an agricultural commodity-driven economy, due to its position as the first major port along the River Shannon. By the middle of the 18th century, Limerick Port grew to become one of Ireland's principal commercial ports, exporting agricultural produce from the most fertile tracts in Ireland known as the [Golden Vale](/wiki/Golden_Vale "Golden Vale") as well as produce from the surrounding counties.[[17]](#cite_note-libraryireland.com-17) The city was one of the main meat processing areas in Ireland, and industries included confectionery and flour production. The city was known for its bacon industry, including the production of [Limerick ham](/wiki/Limerick_ham "Limerick ham") – however this went into decline in the mid-20th century. The fishing industry in Limerick, based on Clancy's Strand opposite [King John's Castle](/wiki/King_John%27s_Castle_(Limerick) "King John's Castle (Limerick)") and at Coonagh nearby, once employed hundreds of men.[[68]](#cite_note-68) The boat most commonly used was the [Gandelow](/wiki/Gandelow "Gandelow") which was also used as a small [Lighter (barge)](/wiki/Lighter_(barge) "Lighter (barge)") to ferry goods to and from larger ships in the port.[[69]](#cite_note-69) In the 1920s the construction of a dam at [Ardnacrusha](/wiki/Ardnacrusha "Ardnacrusha") severely impacted salmon breeding and that, and the introduction of quotas, had by the 1950s caused salmon fishing to diminish.[[70]](#cite_note-70) By 2006, most fishermen had relinquished their drift net licences and the industry can now be said to be dormant.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

As with other cities in Ireland, several multinational companies are based in Limerick, and the city is a growing recipient of foreign direct investment.[[72]](#cite_note-72) [Dell](/wiki/Dell_Inc. "Dell Inc.") had its main European Manufacturing Facility at the Raheen Business Park however in January 2009 Dell announced that it would close its Limerick computer manufacturing plant and move the production lines to Poland.[[73]](#cite_note-73) The facility was the largest Dell manufacturing plant outside the United States and produced 30,000–60,000 units per day for export to the [EMEA](/wiki/Europe,_the_Middle_East_and_Africa "Europe, the Middle East and Africa"). Dell remains one of the largest employers in the mid-west with over 1,000 people employed in service and support.[[74]](#cite_note-74) [Analog Devices](/wiki/Analog_Devices "Analog Devices") has its European manufacturing base in Raheen, 3 km south-west of the city centre. The site employs more than 1,000 people. [Johnson & Johnson](/wiki/Johnson_%26_Johnson "Johnson & Johnson") subsidiary [Vistakon](/wiki/Vistakon "Vistakon") (the world's largest manufacturer of [contact lenses](/wiki/Contact_lenses "Contact lenses")) has a large facility in [Castletroy](/wiki/Castletroy "Castletroy") in the [National Technology Park](/wiki/Plassey,_County_Limerick "Plassey, County Limerick") and also employs close to 1,000 people. It is Vistakon's only production facility outside the United States and one of the largest contact-lens manufacturing plants in the world. Cook Medical, the world's largest privately owned Medical Devices company, employs over 800 people in Limerick at the [National Technology Park](/wiki/Plassey,_County_Limerick "Plassey, County Limerick").

The [Post-2008 Irish economic downturn](/wiki/Post-2008_Irish_economic_downturn "Post-2008 Irish economic downturn") had a profound effect on Limerick. The announcement in 2009 that Dell was to move its manufacturing facility from Limerick to Poland devastated the local economy. 1,900 jobs were lost at Dell and it is believed that for every job that was lost at Dell at least another 4 to 5 were at risk. The closure of the Dell manufacturing facility amounted to 2% of Ireland's national GDP.[[75]](#cite_note-75) The downturn in the construction industry also cost many jobs as did the stalled Limerick regeneration programme which promised investment in Limerick's deprived city areas. As of 2012 unemployment had become a major problem across the city with the unemployment rate in the city at 28.6% which was almost twice the national average.[[27]](#cite_note-limerickleader.ie-27)

### Retail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Retail")]

The [Crescent Shopping Centre](/wiki/Crescent_Shopping_Centre "Crescent Shopping Centre") is the largest shopping centre in Ireland, outside of Dublin, and the largest in the province of Munster. It is located in [Dooradoyle](/wiki/Dooradoyle "Dooradoyle") about 3 km south of the city centre, and has over 90 shopping outlets along with restaurants and a 12-screen Omniplex Cinema. The city centre also has a large retail district which includes a mix of more traditional type of shops as well as some modern [high street](/wiki/High_street "High street") stores. [Cruises Street](/wiki/Cruises_Street,_Limerick "Cruises Street, Limerick") is one of the main shopping streets. [O'Connell Street](/wiki/O%27Connell_Street,_Limerick "O'Connell Street, Limerick"), [William Street](/wiki/William_Street,_Limerick "William Street, Limerick"), [Bedford Row](/wiki/Bedford_Row_(Limerick) "Bedford Row (Limerick)") and [Thomas Street](/wiki/Thomas_Street,_Limerick "Thomas Street, Limerick")) are also retail streets within the city. The city centre saw some works on remodeling in the early 21st century, with pedestrianisation works on Bedford Row, Thomas Street and parts of Catherine Street, as well as widening footpaths on William Street.[[76]](#cite_note-76)[[77]](#cite_note-77) These works have sought to address a reduction of footfall in the city centre (in turn influencing the closure of many city centre retail businesses) which occurred from the recession of 2008 onwards.[[78]](#cite_note-78)

#### Retail parks and shopping centres

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Retail parks and shopping centres")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/DunnesStore_Limerick.JPG/250px-DunnesStore_Limerick.JPG)](/wiki/File:DunnesStore_Limerick.JPG)

[Dunnes Stores](/wiki/Dunnes_Stores "Dunnes Stores") in Limerick City

During the [Celtic Tiger](/wiki/Celtic_Tiger "Celtic Tiger") years a number of shopping centres and retail parks opened in suburban areas of Limerick. In addition to the Crescent Shopping Centre, which expanded in the early 2000s, the Jetland Shopping Centre and Childers Road Retail Park opened in the mid-2000s. As of 2017, unfinished retail centres included the [Coonagh](/wiki/Coonagh,_Limerick_City "Coonagh, Limerick City") Cross retail development[[79]](#cite_note-79) and the Parkway Valley Shopping Centre on the Dublin Road.[[80]](#cite_note-80)[[81]](#cite_note-81)

The plans for the Opera Centre, a proposed retail development described as "one of the biggest commercial development sites in the city's history", make up part of the "Limerick 2030" scheme.[[82]](#cite_note-82)[[83]](#cite_note-83)

#### The Milk Market

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: The Milk Market")]

[The Milk Market](/wiki/The_Milk_Market "The Milk Market"), located at Cornmarket Row in the city centre, sells locally produced foods and products and is governed by Limerick Market Trustees.[[84]](#cite_note-84) In 2010 work was undertaken to redevelop the existing premises to an all-weather, all year round facility as it operated in an outdoor environment. The work involved constructing a large canopy over the existing market premises and was officially re-opened in June 2010.[[85]](#cite_note-85) The Milk Market Project won the 2011 [Royal Institute of the Architects of Ireland](/wiki/Royal_Institute_of_the_Architects_of_Ireland "Royal Institute of the Architects of Ireland") (RIAI) 'Peoples Choice Award'.[[86]](#cite_note-86)

### Energy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Energy")]

In 2013, a renewable energy strategy was launched for the region, and proposed a 20% reduction in CO2-emissions, while ensuring that short-term actions were beneficial to the long-term goal of [100% renewable energy](/wiki/100%25_renewable_energy "100% renewable energy"). The strategy was completed by the Danish [Aalborg University](/wiki/Aalborg_University "Aalborg University").[[87]](#cite_note-87)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Transport")]

### Air

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Air")]

[Shannon Airport](/wiki/Shannon_Airport "Shannon Airport") is 20 km west of the city in [County Clare](/wiki/County_Clare "County Clare"). It is one of Ireland's main airports and is accessible from Limerick via the [N18](/wiki/N18_road_(Ireland) "N18 road (Ireland)") dual carriageway and from other regions via the [Limerick Tunnel](/wiki/Limerick_Tunnel "Limerick Tunnel"). It has scheduled flights to European and North American destinations. There is no rail link to the airport. Coonagh airfield, a few kilometres west of [Caherdavin](/wiki/Caherdavin "Caherdavin"), serves small private aircraft. [Kerry](/wiki/Kerry_Airport "Kerry Airport") and [Cork Airports](/wiki/Cork_Airport "Cork Airport") are around 1 hour 30 minutes and 2 hours drive away, respectively. [Kerry Airport](/wiki/Kerry_Airport "Kerry Airport") is connected via [Farranfore railway station](/wiki/Farranfore_railway_station "Farranfore railway station") from [Limerick railway station](/wiki/Limerick_railway_station "Limerick railway station"), and [Irish Citylink](/wiki/Irish_Citylink "Irish Citylink") provide a direct service to Cork Airport[[88]](#cite_note-88)
.

### Bus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Bus")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Bus_Limerick.jpg/250px-Bus_Limerick.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bus_Limerick.jpg)

City Centre bus service

Local public transport is provided by several companies. [Bus Éireann](/wiki/Bus_%C3%89ireann "Bus Éireann"), Ireland's state-owned bus company, operates a number of services subsidised by the National Transport Authority, while a number of provide operators also run commercial bus services in and around Limerick. An updated city bus network was introduced in Limerick City in December 2016,[[89]](#cite_note-89) which introduced new routes.[[90]](#cite_note-90) In May 2019, Bus Éireann further changed and improved the Limerick City timetable, including increased frequency and later operating times.

Other rural buses run to towns and villages in the county and to [Shannon Airport](/wiki/Shannon_Airport "Shannon Airport"). There are also a number of Intercity and international buses from Bus Éireann's [bus terminal](/wiki/Bus_station "Bus station") adjoining [Limerick Colbert railway station](/wiki/Limerick_railway_station "Limerick railway station"). These include hourly services to [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin"), [Cork](/wiki/Cork_(city) "Cork (city)"), [Ennis](/wiki/Ennis "Ennis") and [Galway](/wiki/Galway "Galway"). Expressway runs the 343 bus from Limerick Colbert Station to Shannon Airport. Bus Éireann also run a Limerick to Galway Express service traveling on the [M18](/wiki/M18_motorway_(Ireland) "M18 motorway (Ireland)") in addition to the regular service. Other buses run every 2 hours to [Tralee](/wiki/Tralee "Tralee") and [Killarney](/wiki/Killarney "Killarney"). There are also regular daily services to [Waterford](/wiki/Waterford "Waterford") and [Athlone](/wiki/Athlone "Athlone"), and a daily service to London via the ferry from [Rosslare Europort](/wiki/Rosslare_Europort "Rosslare Europort").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

There are some private bus companies in operation providing city and intercity services. Dublin Coach provides the [M7](/wiki/M7_motorway_(Ireland) "M7 motorway (Ireland)") express service which operates every hour from Arthur's Quay which goes to Dublin or Ennis.[[91]](#cite_note-91) JJ Kavanagh also provide Dublin to Limerick services from Arthur's Quay. Citylink runs a number of services from Limerick to Galway, Cork and Cork Airport and operate from Henry Street.

### Rail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Rail")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Limerick-0103.jpg/250px-Limerick-0103.jpg)](/wiki/File:Limerick-0103.jpg)

Colbert station, Parnell Street

[Iarnród Éireann](/wiki/Iarnr%C3%B3d_%C3%89ireann "Iarnród Éireann")'s [Limerick Colbert station](/wiki/Limerick_railway_station "Limerick railway station") is the railway hub for the city and the Mid-West Region with a number of intercity and commuter rail services. [Limerick railway station](/wiki/Limerick_railway_station "Limerick railway station") opened on 28 August 1858, replacing an earlier temporary station a short distance away, which had operated from 9 May 1848.[[92]](#cite_note-92)

The following [inter city](/wiki/InterCity_(Iarnr%C3%B3d_%C3%89ireann) "InterCity (Iarnród Éireann)") routes are available from [Limerick Colbert station](/wiki/Limerick_railway_station "Limerick railway station") :

* **Limerick to Dublin Heuston**: 16 services daily including 4 direct services. All remaining Dublin – Limerick services require passengers to change to the [Dublin to Cork](/wiki/Dublin-Cork_railway_line "Dublin-Cork railway line") train at either [Limerick Junction](/wiki/Limerick_Junction_railway_station "Limerick Junction railway station") or at [Ballybrophy railway station](/wiki/Ballybrophy_railway_station "Ballybrophy railway station").
* **[Limerick to Waterford](/wiki/Limerick-Rosslare_railway_line "Limerick-Rosslare railway line")**: 2 services daily (but none on Sundays). Passengers must change train at [Limerick Junction](/wiki/Limerick_Junction_railway_station "Limerick Junction railway station") to continue to Waterford with stations at [Tipperary](/wiki/Tipperary_railway_station "Tipperary railway station"), [Cahir](/wiki/Cahir_railway_station "Cahir railway station"), [Clonmel](/wiki/Clonmel_railway_station "Clonmel railway station") and [Carrick-on-Suir](/wiki/Carrick-on-Suir "Carrick-on-Suir").
* **[Limerick to Galway](/wiki/Western_Railway_Corridor "Western Railway Corridor")**: 5 services daily. This service reopened on 29 March 2010.[[93]](#cite_note-93)
* **Limerick to Cork**: Passengers can travel between Limerick and [Cork](/wiki/Cork_Kent_railway_station "Cork Kent railway station") via [Limerick Junction](/wiki/Limerick_Junction_railway_station "Limerick Junction railway station"). There is no direct service.
* **Limerick to Killarney, Farranfore and Tralee**: Passengers can travel between Limerick and [Killarney](/wiki/Killarney_railway_station "Killarney railway station"), [Farranfore](/wiki/Farranfore_railway_station "Farranfore railway station") and [Tralee](/wiki/Tralee_railway_station "Tralee railway station") via [Limerick Junction](/wiki/Limerick_Junction_railway_station "Limerick Junction railway station") where trains run via [Mallow](/wiki/Mallow_railway_station "Mallow railway station") to reach County Kerry.

There are 3 suburban/commuter services in the [Limerick Suburban Rail](/wiki/Limerick_Suburban_Rail "Limerick Suburban Rail") network:

* **Limerick – Ennis** calling at [Sixmilebridge](/wiki/Sixmilebridge "Sixmilebridge")
* **Limerick – Nenagh** calling at [Castleconnell](/wiki/Castleconnell "Castleconnell") and [Birdhill](/wiki/Birdhill "Birdhill") (with some services continuing to [Cloughjordan](/wiki/Cloughjordan "Cloughjordan") & [Roscrea](/wiki/Roscrea "Roscrea") & connecting to the [Dublin-Cork railway line](/wiki/Dublin-Cork_railway_line "Dublin-Cork railway line") at [Ballybrophy](/wiki/Ballybrophy_railway_station "Ballybrophy railway station").) Due to speed restrictions, this alternative Dublin – Limerick route can take 60 minutes longer (with a change at [Ballybrophy](/wiki/Ballybrophy_railway_station "Ballybrophy railway station")) than by [Limerick Junction](/wiki/Limerick_Junction_railway_station "Limerick Junction railway station") albeit a more direct service.
* **Limerick – [Limerick Junction](/wiki/Limerick_Junction_railway_station "Limerick Junction railway station") and [Thurles](/wiki/Thurles_railway_station "Thurles railway station")**

There are also some disused railway lines including the [Limerick to Foynes line](/wiki/Limerick%E2%80%93Foynes_railway_line "Limerick–Foynes railway line") which closed to freight in the early 2000s, having lost passenger services in the early 1960s, although the track is still in situ. This is the last remaining part of the North Kerry Line which closed to passenger traffic in the early 1960s and to freight traffic (other than Limerick – Foynes) in the mid-1970s.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In 2022, [Iarnród Éireann](/wiki/Iarnr%C3%B3d_%C3%89ireann "Iarnród Éireann") commenced works to reopen the former Limerick–Foynes line for freight traffic.[[94]](#cite_note-94) As of 2024, these works were projected to be completed by 2025, with freight operations due to commence from early 2026.[[95]](#cite_note-95)

### Road

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Road")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Limerick_-_Thomond_Bridge_-_geograph.org.uk_-_331738.jpg/250px-Limerick_-_Thomond_Bridge_-_geograph.org.uk_-_331738.jpg)](/wiki/File:Limerick_-_Thomond_Bridge_-_geograph.org.uk_-_331738.jpg)

Thomond Bridge over the River Shannon

Limerick's central location in the midwest of Ireland means many national primary routes converge on the city.[[96]](#cite_note-96) The [M7](/wiki/M7_motorway_(Ireland) "M7 motorway (Ireland)") (Dublin), [N/M18](/wiki/N18_road_(Ireland) "N18 road (Ireland)") (Galway, Ennis, Shannon), [N/M20](/wiki/N20_road_(Ireland) "N20 road (Ireland)") (Cork), [N21](/wiki/N21_road_(Ireland) "N21 road (Ireland)") (Tralee) and [N24](/wiki/N24_road_(Ireland) "N24 road (Ireland)") (Waterford) routes all start/terminate in or near the city. Road infrastructure is further supplemented by the southern ring road and [Limerick Tunnel](/wiki/Limerick_Tunnel "Limerick Tunnel") bypass of the city and the M20 bypass of Dooradoyle and Raheen to the south of the city. Connections to the other cities were improved with the completion of the [M7 motorway](/wiki/M7_motorway_(Ireland) "M7 motorway (Ireland)") in December 2010, and continuing upgrades ongoing to the N/M18 to Shannon, Ennis, Galway, and Tuam – the final section opened in September 2017.[[97]](#cite_note-97) A motorway is also planned between Cork and Limerick, with a proposed completion date in the mid-2020s.[[98]](#cite_note-98)

### Waterway transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Waterway transport")]

Historically, waterway transport has been key to Limerick's development. Vikings established the city as a maritime trading port. The city's position on a major Irish river system, the [Shannon](/wiki/River_Shannon "River Shannon"), enabled transport to the midlands of Ireland and further north and west. By the 18th century with the opening of canal systems throughout Ireland, Limerick Port established itself as Ireland's premier commercial port on the western side of the country. This was enabled by the ease of access facilitated by the opening of canals but most importantly to the River Shannon. The opening of the [Grand Canal](/wiki/Grand_Canal_(Ireland) "Grand Canal (Ireland)") in the 18th century provided further access to Dublin and the east of the country. There are also a number of disused canals in the vicinity of the city including the Park Canal & the Plassey-Errina Canal on the old Limerick navigation. Waterway transport on the Shannon was regularly used to transport goods from Limerick to Dublin and vice versa however this mode of transport fell into decline in the 20th century. Originally Limerick port was located near the confluence of the Abbey and Shannon rivers at [King's Island](/wiki/King%27s_Island,_Limerick "King's Island, Limerick"). Today the [Shannon Foynes Port](/wiki/Shannon_Foynes_Port "Shannon Foynes Port") is located further downstream on the Shannon alongside the Dock Road. This general-purpose facility port is operated by the Shannon Foynes Port Company which operates all marine activities in the Shannon estuary.[[99]](#cite_note-99)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/University_of_Limerick_Plassey_House.jpeg/250px-University_of_Limerick_Plassey_House.jpeg)](/wiki/File:University_of_Limerick_Plassey_House.jpeg)

Plassey House at the [University of Limerick](/wiki/University_of_Limerick "University of Limerick").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/LITPanorama.JPG/250px-LITPanorama.JPG)](/wiki/File:LITPanorama.JPG)

[Limerick Institute of Technology](/wiki/Limerick_Institute_of_Technology "Limerick Institute of Technology")

Limerick is a centre of higher education in the region, and technical and continuation education within the city traces its beginning back to the formation of the [Limerick Athenaeum](/wiki/Limerick_Athenaeum "Limerick Athenaeum") Society in 1852, marking the foundation of the [Limerick Institute of Technology](/wiki/Limerick_Institute_of_Technology "Limerick Institute of Technology"). The Society's aims included "the promotion of Literature, Science, Art and Music".[[100]](#cite_note-100) Limerick is now home to a number of higher-education institutions including the [University of Limerick](/wiki/University_of_Limerick "University of Limerick"), [Limerick Institute of Technology](/wiki/Limerick_Institute_of_Technology "Limerick Institute of Technology") (one of the two constituent campuses of the [Technological University of the Shannon: Midlands Midwest](/wiki/Technological_University_of_the_Shannon:_Midlands_Midwest "Technological University of the Shannon: Midlands Midwest")) and [Mary Immaculate College](/wiki/Mary_Immaculate_College "Mary Immaculate College") and has a student population of over 20,000.[[101]](#cite_note-101)

The University of Limerick (UL) has a student population of over 17,000 and is about 5 km east of the city centre, in the suburb of [Castletroy](/wiki/Castletroy "Castletroy"). It was established as the National Institute for Higher Education (NIHE) in 1972, and in 1989 was the first university to be established since the foundation of the [State](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland") in 1922. Its academic programs include courses in engineering, information technology, [materials science](/wiki/Materials_science "Materials science"), [sports science](/wiki/Sports_science "Sports science"), humanities, teacher education, social sciences and music. In 2007, the university opened a medical school. The Irish World Music Centre specialises in traditional music and dance, and UL is host to the [Irish Chamber Orchestra](/wiki/Irish_Chamber_Orchestra "Irish Chamber Orchestra"). The campus includes a 50m Olympic-standard swimming complex, the first to be established in Ireland.[[102]](#cite_note-102) The campus has one of the longest footbridges in Europe, "[The Living Bridge](/wiki/The_Living_Bridge "The Living Bridge")".[[103]](#cite_note-103)[[104]](#cite_note-104) [Thomond College of Education, Limerick](/wiki/Thomond_College_of_Education,_Limerick "Thomond College of Education, Limerick") was a teacher training college for secondary level and was integrated into the university in 1991.

Limerick Institute of Technology (LIT) has a student population of approximately 7,000 and is a centre for undergraduate and postgraduate education in business, engineering, information technology, humanities, science, and art education. In October 2021, it merged with [Athlone IT](/wiki/Athlone_IT "Athlone IT") to become the [Technological University of the Shannon: Midlands Midwest](/wiki/Technological_University_of_the_Shannon:_Midlands_Midwest "Technological University of the Shannon: Midlands Midwest"), in 2021/2022.[[105]](#cite_note-105)[[106]](#cite_note-106) The main campus is located at Moylish Park, about 3 kilometres north-west of the city centre, and the [Limerick School of Art and Design](/wiki/Limerick_School_of_Art_and_Design "Limerick School of Art and Design") is located on campuses at Clare Street and George's Quay. Additional facilities and outreach centres are located at O'Connell Street and in Ennis, County Clare and LIT has two campuses in County Tipperary, LIT Thurles and LIT Clonmel. These were formerly the campuses of the [Tipperary Institute](/wiki/Tipperary_Institute "Tipperary Institute") which merged with LIT in 2011.[[107]](#cite_note-107) LIT was originally established in 1852 as a School of Ornamental Art. In the mid-1970s it was incorporated as the Limerick College of Art, Commerce & Technology (CoACT) and achieved the designation of a Regional Technical College (RTC) in 1993 and finally an [Institute of Technology](/wiki/Institutes_of_Technology_in_Ireland "Institutes of Technology in Ireland") in 1997. LIT has connections with several enterprise centres.[[108]](#cite_note-108)

Mary Immaculate College is an education and arts college located just southwest of the city centre. The main focus is on the education of primary-level teachers. Mary Immaculate College is a constituent college of the [University of Limerick](/wiki/University_of_Limerick "University of Limerick").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Griffith College Limerick](/wiki/Griffith_College_Limerick "Griffith College Limerick") (GCL) is a private college in Limerick. The college was established in 2006 when the Mid-West Business Institute was acquired by Griffith College. The college runs full-time and part-time courses in accountancy, business, law, engineering, computing, and IT.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Primary and secondary education in the city is organised similarly to the rest of Ireland. The Limerick and Clare Education and Training Board (formerly the City of Limerick Vocational Education Committee) provides education services for over 25,000 learners in the city at secondary and further education levels. Secondary level schools in Limerick include [Castletroy College](/wiki/Castletroy_College "Castletroy College"), [Crescent College](/wiki/Crescent_College "Crescent College") Comprehensive, Thomond Community College, [Villiers](/wiki/Villiers_Secondary_School "Villiers Secondary School"), Educate Together, and the all-Irish language Gaelcholáiste Luimnigh, which are all [co-educational](/wiki/Coeducation "Coeducation"). [Ardscoil Rís](/wiki/Ardscoil_R%C3%ADs,_Limerick "Ardscoil Rís, Limerick"), St. Clements Redemptorist College, [CBS Sexton Street](/wiki/CBS_Sexton_Street "CBS Sexton Street") and [St Munchin's College](/wiki/St_Munchin%27s_College "St Munchin's College") are boys-only schools, and [Laurel Hill Coláiste](/wiki/Laurel_Hill_Col%C3%A1iste "Laurel Hill Coláiste"), Laurel Hill Secondary School, Coláiste Nano Nagle and Ardscoil Mhuire are girls-only schools.[[109]](#cite_note-live952-109)

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Sport")]

[Rugby](/wiki/Rugby_Union "Rugby Union"), [Gaelic football](/wiki/Gaelic_football "Gaelic football"), [hurling](/wiki/Hurling "Hurling"), and association football are popular sporting pastimes in Limerick. The city and suburbs also have many tennis, athletics, [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket") and golf clubs. The city is host to many large sporting events. Examples in the 21st century include the 2008 and 2009 Irish Open Golf Championships, the 2010 Irish [Special Olympics](/wiki/Special_Olympics "Special Olympics"), the All-Ireland Corporate Games, and the World [Baton twirling](/wiki/Baton_twirling "Baton twirling") Championships.[[110]](#cite_note-110) Limerick was designated as a European City of Sport for 2011 by the European Capitals of Sport Association (ACES).[[111]](#cite_note-111)
The National Elite Swimming Club is based at The University of Limerick Arena. Former World Boxing Champion, [Andy Lee](/wiki/Andy_Lee_(boxer) "Andy Lee (boxer)"), who held the [WBO](/wiki/World_Boxing_Organization "World Boxing Organization") [middleweight](/wiki/Middleweight "Middleweight") title from 2014 to 2015, trained at St. Francis Boxing Club on Mungret Street in Limerick.[[112]](#cite_note-112)

### Basketball

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Basketball")]

Similar to the rest of Ireland, basketball was a popular sport in Limerick during the 1970s and 1980s, with up to four divisions in the men's and women's local leagues. It suffered a decline during the 1990s culminating in the complete demise of local league basketball in the city and surrounding areas. The main clubs in the city were St. Colm's and Marathon with St. Colm's, in particular, having a long history in the National Leagues.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Limerick is currently represented in the [National Leagues](/wiki/Premier_League_(Ireland) "Premier League (Ireland)") by the men's [UL Eagles](/wiki/UL_Eagles "UL Eagles") team and the women's UL Aughinish team. They both play their home games at the 2,500-capacity University Arena at the University of Limerick. There are several other teams at the school and club level, including St. Colm's, Limerick Lakers, Taste of Europe, Limerick Celtic, and [Limerick Lions](/wiki/Limerick_Lions "Limerick Lions").

### Cricket

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Cricket")]

[Limerick Cricket Club](/wiki/Limerick_Cricket_Club "Limerick Cricket Club") is a member of the Munster Cricket Union and plays in competitions organised by the Union. The club has in the past provided players for the [Ireland national cricket team](/wiki/Ireland_national_cricket_team "Ireland national cricket team").

### Rowing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Rowing")]

Four rowing clubs are located in the city, namely [Limerick Boat Club](/wiki/Limerick_Boat_Club "Limerick Boat Club"), Shannon Rowing Club, [St Michael's Rowing Club](/wiki/St_Michael%27s_Rowing_Club "St Michael's Rowing Club"), and Athlunkard Boat Club. St Michael's member and Limerick native [Sam Lynch](/wiki/Sam_Lynch "Sam Lynch") won the [World Rowing Championships](/wiki/World_Rowing_Championships "World Rowing Championships") gold medal in the Men's Lightweight Single Sculls in 2001 and 2002.

### Gaelic games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Gaelic games")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/d/d4/Sporting_Limerick_Logo.jpg/250px-Sporting_Limerick_Logo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sporting_Limerick_Logo.jpg)

Limerick jersey with Sporting Limerick Logo

Ireland's national sports of [hurling](/wiki/Hurling "Hurling") and [Gaelic football](/wiki/Gaelic_football "Gaelic football") are widely played in the city and its surrounding suburbs. The [Limerick county hurling team](/wiki/Limerick_county_hurling_team "Limerick county hurling team") have won five All-Ireland senior hurling championships since 2018 and are [fourth most successful historically](/wiki/All-Ireland_Senior_Hurling_Championship_records_and_statistics#General_performances "All-Ireland Senior Hurling Championship records and statistics").

[Na Piarsaigh](/wiki/Na_Piarsaigh_GAA_(Limerick) "Na Piarsaigh GAA (Limerick)") is the only city club [currently](/wiki/2022_Limerick_Senior_Hurling_Championship "2022 Limerick Senior Hurling Championship") playing hurling at senior level. [Claughaun](/wiki/Claughaun_GAA "Claughaun GAA") (Clochán), Monaleen (Móin a'Lín) and Mungret (Mungairit) compete at intermediate level and Old Christians (Sean-Chríostaithe), Milford (Áth an Mhuilinn), Saint Patrick's (Naomh Pádraig) and Abbey Sarsfields (Sáirséalaigh na Mainistreach) compete at junior level.

Limerick won the first [All-Ireland Senior Football Championship](/wiki/All-Ireland_Senior_Football_Championship "All-Ireland Senior Football Championship") in 1887 when represented by the city's Commercials club and repeated the feat in 1896. Since then, the game has lived mostly in the shadow of hurling but a resurgence in 2000 saw the county win its first Munster Under-21 title and has since reached three Munster Senior finals. Monaleen (Móin a'Lín) is the only city club to play football in the senior grade. Saint Patrick's (Naomh Pádraig), Claughaun (An Clochán), Mungret St. Paul's (Mungairit Naomh Pól) and [Na Piarsaigh](/wiki/Na_Piarsaigh_GAA_(Limerick) "Na Piarsaigh GAA (Limerick)") are at intermediate level and Milford (Áth a Mhuilinn), Abbey Sarsfields (Sáirséalaigh na Mainstreach) and Ballinacurra Gaels (Gaeil Bhaile na Cora) play at junior level.

Some secondary school's compete in the [Dr. Harty Cup](/wiki/Dr._Harty_Cup "Dr. Harty Cup"), which is the Munster Colleges Hurling Championship. Limerick CBS has won the cup on 10 occasions, including four in a row from 1964 to 1967 and most recently in 1993. The school also won the [Dr. Croke Cup](/wiki/Dr._Croke_Cup "Dr. Croke Cup"), the All-Ireland Colleges Hurling Championship, on two occasions, in 1964 and 1966. Ardscoil Rís has won the championship on five occasions, in 2010, 2011, 2013,2015, and 2021 and St. Munchin's College won it once, in 1922.

Both the University of Limerick (UL) and Limerick Institute of Technology (LIT) have been successful in the [Fitzgibbon Cup](/wiki/Fitzgibbon_Cup "Fitzgibbon Cup"), the All-Ireland Higher Education Hurling Championship. UL first won the championship in 1989 and has won it four times in all. LIT's two wins came in 2005 and 2007. Both of the colleges met in the final in 2011, with UL scoring an injury-time goal to win.[[113]](#cite_note-113)

Limerick's [Gaelic Grounds](/wiki/Gaelic_Grounds "Gaelic Grounds") (Páirc na nGael), on the Ennis Road, is the county team's home venue for both sports and has a capacity of 49,000 following reconstruction in 2004. In 1961 it hosted Ireland's biggest crowd for a sporting event outside [Croke Park](/wiki/Croke_Park "Croke Park") when over 61,000 paid to see the Munster hurling final between Tipperary and Cork.[[114]](#cite_note-114)

### Golf

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Golf")]

There are three golf clubs associated with Limerick city. [Limerick Golf Club](/wiki/Limerick_Golf_Club "Limerick Golf Club") was founded in 1891 and is located at Ballyclough, 5 kilometres (3 mi) due south of the city centre. Castletroy Golf Club was founded in 1937 and is located in the suburb of Castletroy in the southwest of the city. Rathbane Golf Club is based at Rathbane Golf Course, a municipal facility opened in 1998 on the southern outskirts of the city and operated under a licence for Limerick City Council. Limerick has won the Irish Senior Cup, the blue riband event of Irish amateur golf, on four occasions and was the first Irish club to win the European Club Championship, in 1980.[[115]](#cite_note-115) Castletroy has won the Irish Senior Cup once.

Limerick Golf Club was host to the JP McManus Invitational Pro-Am, one of the largest [pro-am](/wiki/Pro-am "Pro-am") events of its kind in the world. It has contributed over €95m to local charities since its inception in 1990.[[116]](#cite_note-116) The event moved to the larger [Adare Golf Club](/wiki/Adare_Golf_Club "Adare Golf Club") in 2005 as it had outgrown the Ballyclough venue.[[117]](#cite_note-117) Adare also played host to the [Irish Open](/wiki/Irish_Open_(golf) "Irish Open (golf)") in 2007 and 2008.

### Rugby

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Rugby")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Munster_rugby_2006.jpg/250px-Munster_rugby_2006.jpg)](/wiki/File:Munster_rugby_2006.jpg)

Munster fans in Limerick during the 2006 Heineken Cup

[Rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") is popular in the city and is widely played at all levels, with Limerick sometimes referred to as the "spiritual home of Irish rugby".[[118]](#cite_note-118)[[119]](#cite_note-119) Two-time European champions [Munster](/wiki/Munster_Rugby "Munster Rugby") play most home matches at [Thomond Park](/wiki/Thomond_Park "Thomond Park"), where they held a record of being unbeaten in the [Heineken Cup](/wiki/Heineken_Cup "Heineken Cup") for 26 consecutive games until the 16–9 defeat by Leicester in January 2007.[[120]](#cite_note-120) Munster recorded a famous 12–0 victory against the New Zealand [All Blacks](/wiki/New_Zealand_national_rugby_union_team "New Zealand national rugby union team") in 1978 at Thomond Park and came close a second time when the teams met in 2008, losing 18–16.[[121]](#cite_note-121) Munster also defeated an Australian touring side at Thomond Park in 2010[[122]](#cite_note-122) and the [Maori All Blacks](/wiki/Maori_All_Blacks "Maori All Blacks") 27–14 in 2016.[[123]](#cite_note-123)

Since its inception in 1991 the [All-Ireland League](/wiki/AIB_League "AIB League") has been dominated by Limerick City teams, with three clubs winning the competition 13 times between them: [Shannon](/wiki/Shannon_RFC "Shannon RFC") (9); [Garryowen](/wiki/Garryowen_Football_Club "Garryowen Football Club") (3) and [Young Munster](/wiki/Young_Munster "Young Munster") (1). Other senior rugby clubs in the city include [Old Crescent](/wiki/Old_Crescent "Old Crescent"), [Thomond](/wiki/Thomond_RFC "Thomond RFC"), and [UL Bohemians](/wiki/UL_Bohemians "UL Bohemians"). Richmond and St. Mary's are city clubs playing in the junior leagues.

The city's secondary schools compete in the [Munster Senior](/wiki/Munster_Schools_Senior_Cup "Munster Schools Senior Cup") and [Junior](/wiki/Munster_Schools_Junior_Cup "Munster Schools Junior Cup") Cups and a number of schools have had notable success at both levels. The most successful rugby school in the city is [Crescent College](/wiki/Crescent_College "Crescent College"), eleven-time Senior Cup and five-time Junior Cup winners. The school is affiliated with Old Crescent RFC. [St. Munchin's](/wiki/St_Munchin%27s_College "St Munchin's College") has won the Senior Cup five times since 1968 and the Junior Cup three times. [Limerick CBS](/wiki/CBS_Sexton_Street "CBS Sexton Street") won the Senior Cup on four occasions in the 1920s and 1930s and the Junior Cup in 1932. [Ardscoil Rís](/wiki/Ardscoil_R%C3%ADs,_Limerick "Ardscoil Rís, Limerick") has won the Junior Cup twice, in 2003 and 2005 and [Castletroy College](/wiki/Castletroy_College "Castletroy College") won both senior and junior competitions in 2008.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Thomond_Park.jpg/250px-Thomond_Park.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thomond_Park.jpg)

[Thomond Park](/wiki/Thomond_Park "Thomond Park") is the home grounds of [Munster Rugby](/wiki/Munster_Rugby "Munster Rugby")

In 2013, [Thomond Park](/wiki/Thomond_Park "Thomond Park") hosted [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") in the [2013 Rugby League World Cup](/wiki/2013_Rugby_League_World_Cup "2013 Rugby League World Cup"),[[124]](#cite_note-124) and an academy was briefly set up in 2011 to identify Irish players to play for [Super League](/wiki/Super_League "Super League") clubs.[[125]](#cite_note-125)[[126]](#cite_note-126) Domestic rugby league was formerly played in Limerick, with the [Treaty City Titans](/wiki/Treaty_City_Titans "Treaty City Titans") representing the city in the [All-Ireland Rugby League Championship](/wiki/All-Ireland_Rugby_League_Championship "All-Ireland Rugby League Championship"). The Titans were Irish champions eight times between 2005 and 2015, but as of 2023 the team is defunct.

### Association football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Association football")]

Association football is popular in the city and suburbs, and the city was historically represented in the [League of Ireland](/wiki/League_of_Ireland "League of Ireland") by [Limerick FC](/wiki/Limerick_F.C. "Limerick F.C."). The club first joined the league in 1937. There have been a number of variations of the club, and their most successful period was from the 1960s to the 1980s when they won two League of Ireland championships and two [FAI Cups](/wiki/FAI_Cup "FAI Cup"). The club played at [Markets Field](/wiki/Markets_Field "Markets Field") until the mid-1980s when they controversially moved to a new venue.[[127]](#cite_note-127) Limerick FC returned to the Markets Field in June 2015, following the purchase of the venue by the Limerick Enterprise Development Partnership (LEDP) from [Bord na gCon](/wiki/Bord_na_gCon "Bord na gCon").[[128]](#cite_note-128) However, the club suffered a financial collapse in 2019 and lost its licence.[[129]](#cite_note-129)[[130]](#cite_note-130)

In place of Limerick FC, a new club called [Treaty United F.C.](/wiki/Treaty_United_F.C. "Treaty United F.C.") was created. The women's team, [Treaty United W.F.C.](/wiki/Treaty_United_W.F.C. "Treaty United W.F.C."), began playing in the 2020 season of the [Women's National League](/wiki/Women%27s_National_League_(Ireland) "Women's National League (Ireland)"). The men's team was not able to begin playing until the 2021 season, joining the [League of Ireland First Division](/wiki/League_of_Ireland_First_Division "League of Ireland First Division").[[131]](#cite_note-131)

### Horse racing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Limerick&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Horse racing")]

[Limerick Racecourse](/wiki/Limerick_Racecourse "Limerick Racecourse") is located 10 km outside the city at Greenmount, Patrickswell and holds [flat](/wiki/Flat_racing "Flat racing") and [National Hunt](/wiki/National_Hunt_racing "National Hunt racing") meetings throughout the year.

The racecourse superseded [Greenpark Racecourse](/wiki/Greenpark_Racecourse "Greenpark Racecourse"), a course inside the city, which closed in 1999 after 130 years of racing.[[132]](#cite_note-132)
